constraints: Consider an array AAA of length NNN. NNN is odd.
Alice and Bob will play the following game on it:
Let BBB be an initially empty array.
Alice and Bob take turns playing, with Alice going first.
On the iii-th turn, the current player will choose element AiA_iAi​, and insert it to either the beginning or the end of BBB.
So, Alice will place A1A_1A1​, then Bob will place A2A_2A2​, then Alice will place A3A_3A3​, and so on.
This continues till all NNN elements have been placed in BBB.
Once the game has finished, Alice's score is defined to be min⁡(B1,BN)\min(B_1, B_N)min(B1​,BN​).
Alice's objective is to maximize her score, while Bob's objective is to minimize Alice's score.
Define f(A)f(A)f(A) to be the final score if both players play optimally, for the starting array AAA.
You are given an array AAA of length NNN, where NNN is odd.
There are QQQ point updates to the array. Each update gives you two integers ppp and XXX, and you are required to set Ap=XA_p = XAp​=X.
Report the value of f(A)f(A)f(A) before any updates are performed, and also after each update.
Updates to AAA are permanent.
Input Format
The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.
The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers NNN and QQQ — the length of AAA and the number of updates, respectively.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​.
The next QQQ lines describe the updates. Each of them contains two space-separated integers ppp and XXX, denoting a point update requiring you to set Ap:=XA_p := XAp​:=X.
Output Format

For each test case, output one line containing Q+1Q+1Q+1 space-separated integers: the first integer should be the value of f(A)f(A)f(A) before any updates, and the iii-th of the next QQQ integers should be the value of f(A)f(A)f(A) after the first iii updates.
NNN is odd.
1≤Ai≤N1 \le A_i \le N1≤Ai​≤N
1≤p,X≤N1 \le p, X \le N1≤p,X≤N
The sum of NNN and the sum of QQQ across all tests each won't exceed 2⋅1052\cdot 10^52⋅105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: The process is as follows.

Initially, we have A=[1,2,3]A = [1, 2, 3]A=[1,2,3], and we want to know f(A)f(A)f(A).

Alice starts, and must place the 111 in BBB. Since BBB is empty, it becomes [1][1][1] no matter what.
Bob then must place the 222 either at the start or at the end. BBB becomes either [1,2][1, 2][1,2] or [2,1][2, 1][2,1] depending on his choice.
Alice must place the 333. If B=[1,2]B = [1, 2]B=[1,2] she can make it [3,1,2][3, 1, 2][3,1,2], and if B=[2,1]B = [2, 1]B=[2,1] she can make it [2,1,3][2, 1, 3][2,1,3]. In either case, min⁡(B1,B3)=2\min(B_1, B_3) = 2min(B1​,B3​)=2, and this is the best Alice can do. So, the answer is 222.


The first update sets A3A_3A3​ to 222, so now A=[1,2,2]A = [1, 2, 2]A=[1,2,2].
Once again, it can be seen that f(A)=2f(A) = 2f(A)=2: Alice can follow the same strategy as above to ensure that both 222's are at the ends, so that min⁡(B1,B3)=2\min(B_1, B_3) = 2min(B1​,B3​)=2 for sure.
The second update sets A2A_2A2​ to 111.
Now, Alice cannot avoid having a 111 at an endpoint no matter what she does, so f(A)=1f(A) = 1f(A)=1.
